title: Efficacy and Safety of Systemic Prophylactic Antibacterials in Mechanically Ventilated Patients: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
one_line_summary: Systemic prophylactic antibacterials probably reduce early and overall VAP incidence in mechanically ventilated patients but probably do not reduce in-hospital **mortality**, with potential benefit in brain injury patients.
key_pearls: Systemic prophylactic antibacterials reduce the incidence of early-onset VAP by **48%** (RR, **0.52**; 95% CI, **0.41–0.66**; moderate certainty).
Systemic prophylactic antibacterials reduce overall VAP incidence by **35%** (RR, **0.65**; 95% CI, **0.55–0.77**; moderate certainty).

Prophylactic antibacterials probably do not reduce in-hospital **mortality** (RR, **0.85**; 95% CI, **0.68–1.06**; moderate certainty) or duration of mechanical ventilation (MD, **–0.32**; 95% CI, **–0.79 to 0.16**; moderate certainty).
Subgroup analysis suggests **significant** VAP reduction in **brain injury patients** (RR, **0.60**; 95% CI, **0.49–0.75**), but not in cardiac arrest patients.
No increased risk of bacterial resistance or adverse events was observed, but safety reporting was incomplete with only **7** RCTs reporting resistance and **4** reporting adverse events.
IV administration offers practical advantages over topical SOD, including systemic distribution and ease of use via existing central venous access.
The **mortality** benefit seen in SDD studies may require the synergistic effect of both systemic and topical antibacterial components.
population: Mechanically ventilated ICU patients ≥16 years old with MV duration ≥48 hours, excluding neutropenia or immunosuppressive therapy

sections.heading: Objectives
sections.content:
- To evaluate the efficacy and safety of systemic prophylactic use of antibacterials in mechanically ventilated patients.
- Previous studies showed SDD reduces VAP incidence and **mortality**, but the isolated role of the IV antibacterial component remains unclear.

sections.heading: Methods
sections.content:
- **Design**: Systematic review and meta-analysis following Cochrane Handbook and PRISMA 2020 guidelines.
- **Registration**: PROSPERO (CRD42022374965).
- **Data Sources**: PubMed, Embase, Cochrane Library from inception to **November 30, 2025**; supplementary search of ClinicalTrials.gov.
- **Inclusion Criteria**: RCTs; patients ≥16 years old with MV ≥48 hours; comparison of systemic prophylactic antibacterials vs. placebo, or systemic + topical vs. topical alone; reporting at least one outcome of interest.
- **Exclusion Criteria**: Non-English literature; neutropenia or immunosuppressive therapy; duplicate publications.
- **Outcomes**:
  - *Primary*: Incidence of early-onset VAP (EO-VAP, ≤4 days of MV), overall VAP incidence, in-hospital **mortality**.
  - *Secondary*: ICU **mortality**, length of hospital stay, ICU length of stay, duration of mechanical ventilation, bacterial resistance, adverse event incidence.
- **Risk of Bias**: Assessed using Cochrane RoB 2 tool.
- **Data Synthesis**: Random-effects model using RevMan 5.4; heterogeneity assessed via *I²* statistic.
- **Subgroup Analyses**: By APACHE score (≤16 or >16), age (≤60 or >60 yr), and specific patient populations.
- **Sensitivity Analysis**: Leave-one-out approach for all outcomes.
- **Evidence Quality**: GRADE methodology.

sections.heading: Results
sections.content:
- **Study Selection**: **16,014** articles identified; **11** RCTs with **5562** patients included (intervention: **2946**; control: **2616**).
- **Patient Populations**: 3 studies in post-cardiac arrest patients; 8 studies with mixed reasons (surgery, trauma, brain injury).
- **Interventions**: 10 studies compared systemic antibacterials vs. placebo/conventional treatment; 1 study compared systemic + topical vs. topical alone.
- **Antibacterial Agents**: Predominantly penicillins and cephalosporins, administered within **24 hours** of MV initiation.
- **Risk of Bias**: 5 studies low risk, 6 studies some risk; 3 open-label trials, 6 with unclear allocation concealment.
- **Primary Outcomes**:
  - *EO-VAP*: **8** RCTs (n=**1400**); RR, **0.52** (95% CI, **0.41–0.66**; *I²*=0%; p<0.00001).
  - *Overall VAP*: **8** RCTs (n=**1497**); RR, **0.65** (95% CI, **0.55–0.77**; *I²*=0%; p<0.00001).
  - *In-hospital **mortality***: **8** RCTs (n=**5243**); RR, **0.85** (95% CI, **0.68–1.06**; *I²*=51%; p=0.15).
  - *ICU **mortality***: **4** RCTs (n=**4131**); RR, **0.97** (95% CI, **0.87–1.09**; *I²*=0%; p=0.65).
- **Secondary Outcomes**:
  - *Length of hospital stay*: MD, **–1.56** (95% CI, **–5.15 to 2.02**; *I²*=42%; p=0.39).
  - *ICU length of stay*: MD, **–1.24** (95% CI, **–2.85 to 0.37**; *I²*=68%; p=0.13).
  - *Duration of MV*: MD, **–0.32** (95% CI, **–0.79 to 0.16**; *I²*=0%; p=0.19).
- **Safety**: 7 RCTs reported no increased bacterial resistance; 4 RCTs reported no **significant** increase in adverse events.
- **Subgroup Analyses**:
  - *Brain injury patients*: **Significant** VAP reduction (RR, **0.60**; 95% CI, **0.49–0.75**).
  - *Cardiac arrest patients*: No **significant** VAP reduction (RR, **0.76**; 95% CI, **0.46–1.27**); single study showed **mortality** reduction (RR, **0.58**; 95% CI, **0.36–0.96**).
- **Sensitivity Analysis**: Pooled results robust; excluding one study (DeSmet 2009) made in-hospital **mortality** **significant** (RR, **0.76**; 95% CI, **0.62–0.93**; p=0.007; *I²*=0%).
- **Publication Bias**: Not assessed (maximum 8 studies per outcome).

sections.heading: GRADE Evidence Quality Assessment
sections.content:
- **Moderate certainty**: EO-VAP incidence, VAP incidence, in-hospital **mortality**, duration of mechanical ventilation.
- **Low certainty**: ICU **mortality**, length of hospital stay, ICU length of stay.
- Downgrades applied for:
  - *Indirectness*: One study included patients with *Pseudomonas aeruginosa* colonization; data transformation from median (IQR) to mean ± SD.
  - *Inconsistency*: *I²* > 50% for in-hospital **mortality** and ICU length of stay.
  - *Imprecision*: Wide CIs and limited number of studies for some outcomes.

sections.heading: Discussion
sections.content:
- Systemic prophylactic antibacterials probably reduce VAP incidence but probably do not reduce all-cause **mortality**.
- The **35%** relative reduction in VAP could translate to **significant** cost savings; VAP attributable costs estimated at **$9,969** per case (U.S.) and **~£10,000** (U.K.).
- Brain injury patients showed **significant** VAP reduction, consistent with prior reviews by Hadley-Brown et al. and Ehrenzeller & Klompas.
- No increased resistance risk observed, potentially due to short antibacterial duration (typically <3 days), limited selective pressure, and low baseline resistance prevalence in study regions.
- IV administration offers practical advantages over SOD: systemic distribution achieves therapeutic concentrations throughout the body; central venous access is already in place in most ICU patients.
- **Mortality** reduction may require the synergistic effect of both systemic and topical components, as seen in SDD studies.
- Limitations: All-cause **mortality** analyzed (VAP-attributable **mortality** not reported); predominantly neurocritical or post-cardiac arrest populations limit generalizability; incomplete safety reporting precludes definitive long-term safety conclusions.

strengths_limitations:
- **Strengths**: Incorporation of multiple RCTs expanding sample size; subgroup analyses investigating preventive effects in different populations; GRADE approach employed to assess evidence quality.
- **Limitations**: Methodological quality varied with three open-label trials and six studies with unclear allocation concealment; restriction to English-language publications may introduce language bias; study populations predominantly neurocritical or post-cardiac arrest patients limiting generalizability to broader ICU cohorts; incomplete reporting of bacterial resistance and adverse events precludes definitive long-term safety conclusions.
